title: Greece Civilization_History of Greece, Volume 01 (of 12) by George Grote
paragraphs: My leisure was not equal to executing any large literary undertaking at that time. It wasn't until within the last three or four years that I have been able to devote myself to the work. Continuous and exclusive labor is necessary, without which, though much can be done to illustrate detached points, no entire or complicated subject can ever be presented in a manner worthy of meeting the public eye. The comparison of the two authors affords, indeed, a striking proof of the progress of sound and enlarged thought. views of the ancient world in our current era For centuries since Mitford's work, philological studies have been conducted with remarkable success in Germany. The stock of facts and documents, relatively scarce, handed down from the ancient world, has been combined and illustrated in a thousand different ways. Although our witnesses cannot be multiplied, we at least have numerous interpreters to interpret, repeat, amplify, and explain their fragmented and unclear testimonies.

He will develop the action of that social system, which, while insuring protection to the mass of freemen, acted as a stimulus to the creative impulses of genius, and left superior minds sufficiently unshackled to soar above religious and political routine, to overshoot their own age, and become teachers of posterity. The lack of reliable sources is a significant issue that cannot be ignored or downplayed, despite the limited availability of materials suitable for historians studying modern kingdoms. Expressions of qualified and hesitant affirmation are repeated until the reader becomes exhausted; meanwhile, the writer himself, who feels this restraint more intensely still, is often tempted to break free from the unseen force that binds him down,—to consolidate the possible and probable into certainty, suppress counterbalancing considerations, and replace half-known realities with a more appealing narrative.

For the truth is, that historical records, properly so called, do not begin until long after this date. Nor will any man, who candidly considers the extreme paucity of attested facts, for two centuries after 776 B.C. C , etc ,[p  The legendary age of the Greeks derives its principal charm and dignity from the Homeric poems. An entire chapter is devoted to these poems, including those in the ancient epic, such as the Iliad and Odyssey. The society and manners of the heroic age, as known from Homer's descriptions and allusions, are also described and critiqued.

I shall interweave in their proper places the important but outlying adventures of the Sicilian and Italian Greeks. I will introduce such occasional notices of Grecian political constitutions, philosophy, poetry, and oratory as are necessary to exhibit the many-sided activity of this people during their brief but brilliant career. After the emergence of Alexander, Greek politics became stagnant and declined, losing its appeal to readers and failing to shape the course of the future world. I venture to warn the reader that there will be many events in the aftermath of Greek politics that they will not understand unless they are familiar with the course of their legendary associations.

I trust that these chapters, being more full of speculation and therefore more open to criticism than any of the others, will thus appear in a more complete and satisfactory form. Although our C has diverged significantly from its Latin counterpart in terms of sound, there remains a valid reason for continuing to employ it in written Latin names. By using this letter, we can accurately reproduce the name visually, even if it does not conform to the original auditory representation. But this is not the case when we employ C to represent the Greek letter K, for we depart here not less from the visible than from the audible original; while we mar the unrivalled euphony of the Greek language by that multiplied sibilation which constitutes its least inviting feature in our own.

The legends of ancient Greece. — Æolus His seven sons and five daughters. — 1 However,  We then depart from the point of view of the original hearers without acquiring a consistent or philosophical point of view of our own. For although some attributes and actions attributed to these persons can be explained by allegory, the entire series and system of them cannot: the theorist who adopts this course of explanation finds that, after one or two simple steps, the path is no longer open, and he must clear a way for himself with gratuitous refinements and conjectures. In legendary chronology, Zeus comes after Kronos and Uranos. However, in terms of Grecian conception, Zeus is considered the primary figure, with Kronos and Uranos being secondary figures who served as precursors to be overthrown and remembered as symbols of their conqueror's power.

Zeus and his brothers Poseidon and Hades have made a division of power. Zeus reserved the air and atmosphere for himself, while Poseidon obtained the sea. Hades was given the underworld or infernal regions. The earth and events that occur on it are shared among them, as well as free access to Mount Olympus. Zeus, along with his brothers and fellow deities, form the current pantheon of gods, acknowledged by Homer and Hesiod as being fully dignified and effective. Eros (Love) was the subducer of both gods and mortals. From Chaos, Erebos and Nyx sprang; from these, Aether and Hemera were born. The Cyclopes were Brontes, Steropes, and Arges,—formidable persons, equally distinguished for strength and for manual skill, so that they made the thunder which afterwards formed the irresistible artillery of Zeus. The Hekatoncheires were Kottos, Briareus, and Gygês, of immense bodily strength.

They were not, however, yet quite secure; for Gaea, intermarrying with Tartarus, gave birth to a new and still more formidable monster called Typhon, of such tremendous properties and promise, that had he been allowed to grow into full development, nothing could have prevented him from vanquishing all rivals and becoming supreme. It has been already stated that in Zeus's family, including his brothers and sisters, as well as their divine offspring, we find the present gods. This refers to those deities, for the most part, who were recognized and worshipped by the Homeric and Hesiodic Greeks. Other deities, not included among the Olympians, seem to be excluded because twelve was a complete number without them. However, some of these deities are not inferior in power and dignity to many of the twelve: Hadês, Hêlios, Hekatê, Dionysos, Lêtô, Diônê, Persephonê, Selênê, Themis, Eôs, Harmonia, the Charites, the Muses, the Eileithyiae, the Moirai, the Oceanids and the Nereids, Proteus, Eidothea, the Nymphs, Leukothea, Phorkys, Æolus, Nemesis. 3

I have presented it briefly as it stands in the Hesiodic Theogonia, because that poem, despite great incoherence and confusion arising from its diverse authorship and age, presents an ancient and genuine attempt to arrange the divine past into a coherent sequence. In revenge for this maltreatment of her sons, Gaea produced fourteen Titans: seven male and seven female. The males were Koeos, Krios, Phorkys, Kronos, Oceanus, Hyperion, and Iapetus. The females were Themis, Tethys, Mnemosyne, Theia, Dionysus, Phoebe, and Rhea. They were named Titans because they avenged their elder brother Uranus for his expulsion. Zeus is hidden in the cave of Nyx, the throne of Phanes himself, accompanied by Aethera and Adrasteia, who tend to and protect him. The armed dance and sonorous instruments of the Kuretes keep his infant cries from being heard by Cronus.

The jealous Hêrê intercepts his career and incites the Titans against him. They smear their faces with plaster and approach him on the throne. Tempting his childish fancy with playthings, they kill him with a sword while he contemplates his reflection in a mirror. During the interval between Hesiod and Onomakritus, a revolution took place in the religious mind of Greece, placing both deities among the front ranks. This remarkable change, occurring as it did during the sixth and a part of the seventh century before the Christian era, may be traced to the influence of communication with Egypt, which only became fully open to the Greeks around 500 BCE.

The Greeks identified these two deities with the great Egyptian Osiris and Isis. As a result, what was borrowed from the Egyptian worship of the two goddesses naturally fell into place within the Greek system. Moreover, the worship of Dionysos under an uncertain name was indigenous to Thrace, similar to the Great Mother's cult in Phrygia and Lydia. It featured violent ecstasies, temporary frenzies, and the clash of noisy instruments, characteristics that would later define it in Greece. From all these various countries, novelties unknown to the Homeric men, found their way into Greek worship. Among them was one that deserved special notice, as it marked the beginning of a new class of ideas in their theology. During the century and a half following the opening of Egypt to the Greeks, the old religion became increasingly tainted by imports from Egypt, Asia Minor, and Thrace.

The Homeric Hymns present to us the religious ideas and legends of the Greeks at an earlier period, when the enthusiastic and mystic tendencies had not yet reached their full development. Dionysos assumes the form of a lion. A bear stands nearby. The lion charges at the captain with a loud roar. In the chaos, the crew leaps overboard in terror. As they struggle to escape, they are transformed into dolphins. In the fanatical impulse that inspired followers of the Asiatic Rhea or Cybelê, or of the Thracian Kotys, there was nothing of spontaneous joy; it was a sacred madness, during which the soul appeared to be surrendered to an external stimulus, accompanied by preternatural strength and a temporary sense of power. This was altogether distinct from the unrestrained hilarity of the original Dionysia, as seen in the rural demes of Attica or in the gay city of Tarentum.

She would have made him immortal, but for the indiscreet curiosity and alarm of Metaneira, who secretly watched over him by night and shrieked with horror at the sight of her child in the flames. The indignant goddess set the infant on the ground, revealing her true nature to Metaneira. Her worn appearance vanished, and she stood revealed in the full majesty of her divine form, radiating a brilliant light that filled the entire house. "Foolish mother," she said, "your lack of faith has cost your son his mortal life." The well, overshadowed by an olive tree near which Dêmêtêr had rested, was a familiar and interesting place for every Eleusinian. The stream Kallichorus and the temple hill were also well-known spots. The Mysts, or communicants, always tasted a peculiar posset made from barley meal with mint after a prescribed fast as part of the ceremony. Additionally, it was customary at a particular spot during the processional march to allow for the free exchange of personal jokes and taunts among individuals for general amusement. In the enlarged representation of the Eleusinian ceremonies, which became established after the incorporation of Eleusis with Athens, a woman or man in women's attire, possessing suitable wit and imagination, played the role of Iambê. She was positioned on the bridge over the Kephissos and addressed the passers-by in the procession, particularly the prominent men of Athens, delivering saucy jeers that were likely as biting as those of Aristophanes on stage.[92]

He crossed the Euripus to Bœotia, passing by Teumessus and Mycalessus, before reaching an unoccupied forest that would later become the site of Thebes. He established his oracle on a mountainous site, untouched by chariots and horses, and close to a fountain that was guarded by a massive serpent, once the nurse of the monster Typhon. He took on the form of a massive dolphin and splashed about, causing the vessel to rock violently as he shook it. The mariners were filled with terror as he sent a strong gust of wind that propelled her along the coast of Peloponnese towards the Corinthian Gulf. Eventually, she ran aground in the harbor of Krissa.

Apollo revealed himself as the author and contriver of their miraculous voyage, telling them about the noble role he had ordained for them. They followed him by his orders to the rocky Pytho on Mount Parnassus, singing the solemn hymn of Io-Paian, as it is sung in Krete, while the god himself marched at their head, with his fine form and lofty step, playing on the lyre. He showed them the temple and the site of the oracle. He then instructed them to worship him as Apollo Delphinios, for it was at this place that he had first appeared to them in the form of a dolphin. But how are we to live in a place with no crops, vines, or pasture? "Ye silly mortals," answered the god, "who seek only toil and privation, know that a more easy lot is yours." The specific functions of a god and the chief localities of his worship, along with the surnames associated with them, are thus historically explained, being connected to his past deeds and exploits.

Though these poems are only interesting to us, they possessed all the requisites of history for those who heard them sung. They were fully believed as such, not because they were partially founded in reality, but because they ran in complete harmony with the feelings. As long as this condition was met, it was not fashionable to canvass truth or falsehood. It is older than the Iliad or Odyssey. In the latter, both Pytho and Delos are mentioned, although Delos is not named in the former. The distribution and partialities of the gods in that poem are different from what they become in later times. This difference cannot be satisfactorily explained by our current understanding.

Apollo had numerous temples throughout Greece, including the Delphian temple. He also had oracles at Abae in Phokis on Mount Ptonos, Tegyra in Boeotia, where he was said to have been born, and Branchidae near Milos. Additionally, he had temples at Klarus in Asia Minor and Patara in Lycia. Even during the third century of the Christian era, in the time of the Greek orator Menander, when the old forms of paganism were waning and mythological stories were extremely abundant, we see this demand in great force; but it was incomparably more operative in earlier times when the creative vein of the Greek mind still retained its pristine richness. Each god had many different names, temples, groves, and rituals; with each of which was connected to a varying degree of mythological narrative, originally conceived in the fertile imagination of a believing community, later developed and disseminated through the poetry of a bard.

Both Christianity and Islam originated during the historical age, have been spread from a single common centre, and have evolved from the ruins of an existing faith. Aphrodite is described in the hymn as cold and unimpressive, yet always active and irresistible in inspiring amorous feelings among gods, men, and animals. It seems that this goddess was conceived differently in various parts of Greece.

The jealousy of the gods at the withholding of honors and sacrifices, or at the presumption of mortals in contending with them, is evident in the legends of Artemis. The memorable Kalydônian boar was sent by her as a punishment upon Œneus, because he had omitted to sacrifice to her, while he honored other gods. The Arcadian heroine Atalanta is a reproduction of Artemis, with little or no difference. She is sometimes confused with her attendant nymphs. Hephaestus's skill and his deformity appear alternately as the source of mythical stories. Whenever exquisite and effective fabrication is intended to be designated, Hephaestus is announced as the maker. However, in this role, the type of his character is reflected in Daedalus. To appease his mother Maia, who warned him about offending Apollo, Hermês replied that he aimed to rival Apollo's dignity and functions among the immortals. If Zeus refused to grant them, he would use his thieving powers to break into the Delphi sanctuary and steal its gold, vestments, precious tripods, and vessels. Apollo discovered the loss of his cattle and, after some trouble, found his way to the Kyllenean cave. There, he saw Hermes sleeping in his cradle.

The child denies the theft with audacity, treating it as an absurd impossibility. Even Zeus, who detects him immediately, forces him to disclose the location where the cattle are hidden. He seeks the gift of prophecy, but Apollo is bound by a special vow not to grant that privilege to any god. Instead, he instructs Hermes on how to glean limited information from the Moirai or Fates; and assigns him the additional role of messenger for Hades. Apollo had acquired the lyre, the object of his desires, but he remained anxious that Hermes would steal it away from him once more, along with his bow. To ensure this did not happen, Apollo demanded a formal oath by the river Styx. Hermes promises solemnly that he will not steal any acquisitions and will never invade the sanctuary of Apollo. In return, Apollo pledges himself to recognize Hermes as his chosen friend and companion among all the other sons of Zeus, both human and divine. So it was decreed by Zeus that Apollo showed favor to Hermes.

To Zeus, more amours are ascribed than to any of the other gods. Probably this is because the Grecian kings and chieftains were eager to establish a direct lineage to the most glorious of all, each of these amours having a corresponding descendant on earth. Such subjects were among the most promising and agreeable for the interest of mythical narrative. Zeus, as a lover, became the father of many legends, branching out into numerous interferences. His sons, all distinguished individuals, and many of whom were persecuted by Hera, furnished the occasion. Zeus decided with both hands and took the white fat. However, upon finding that he had gotten nothing at the bottom except the bones, he became highly enraged. The choice of the gods was now irrevocably made. They were not entitled to any portion of the sacrificed animal beyond the bones and the white fat. This stood as a widely accepted practice, which could be reasonably explained by its origins. I select this as one instance to illustrate the genesis of legend out of religious practices. After death, they became, by the award of Zeus, guardians of the earth, watching over humanity's affairs without being seen. With royal authority, they dispense wealth and keep track of both good and evil deeds. The gods created the silver race, which was unlike and greatly inferior to the golden race, both in terms of their minds and bodies. The concept of the golden race passing into good guardian dæmons after death is often compared to oriental angels. Some believe it was inspired by a comparison between these two entities. This idea presents itself to the poet in two ways: as a means of elevating the golden race to godlike status, and as a way to establish a hierarchical structure for post-mortem existence, with each group's level of existence corresponding to its character during life.

But the chiefs who ruled before Thêbes and Troy could not be well identified either with the golden, silver, or bronze race: it was essential that they should be placed in immediate proximity to the present race, because their descendants, real or supposed, were among the most prominent and conspicuous of existing men. His poem is remarkable as the most ancient didactic production of the Greeks, and as one of the first signs of a new tone of sentiment that was finding its way into their literature, which would never afterwards become extinct. He turns away in disgust from the present men, not because they lack the strength to wield even the mighty spear of Achilles or a massive boundary stone, but because they are greedy, cunning, and morally bankrupt.

The dæmons were first introduced into the religious atmosphere of the Grecian world by the author of "Works and Days," depicted as being distinct from the gods but fundamentally good, serving as intermediaries between the divine and human realms. They warrant attention as the seed of a doctrine that underwent significant transformations, eventually becoming a crucial component of pagan faith before contributing to its eventual subversion. After his death, his two sons divided the family inheritance. However, Hesiod bitterly complained that his brother Perses had cheated and taken him to court, where he obtained an unjust verdict from corrupt judges. The sons of the Titan god Iapetus, as described in the Hesiodic theogony, are Atlas, Menoetius, Prometheus, and Epimetheus. Of the gods, Atlas is the only one mentioned by Homer in the Odyssey. He is not referred to as the son of Iapetus, but rather Iapetus is mentioned as being in Tartarus alongside Cronus.

Atlas was condemned by Zeus to support the heavens on his head and hands, rather than being the keeper of the intermediate pillars between heaven and earth. In contrast, the fierce Menoeus was punished by being cast into the abyss of Erebus for his unruly behavior. Prometheus and Epimetheus (the forethinker and the aftethinker) are characters stamped at the same time and by the same effort, embodying the express contrast and antithesis of each other. Hephaestus, by his direction, molded the form of a beautiful virgin. Athena dressed her, Aphrodite and the Graces bestowed upon her both ornament and fascination, while Hermes infused into her the mind of a dog, a deceitful spirit, and treacherous words. The messenger of the gods conducted this fascinating mischief on mankind, at a time when Prometheus was not present.

However, his scheme is thwarted by the theft of Prometheus. His second attempt, however, is more successful, and he deceives the unsuspecting Epimetheus into accepting a gift (despite Prometheus's strict prohibition) that ultimately destroys all of human happiness. But the miseries that arose from women were greater than those experienced by Prometheus himself.

He suppresses both the first trick played off by Prometheus upon Zeus, respecting the partition of the victim, and the final formation and sending of Pandora. This covers the two most significant parts of the Hesiodic story. In contrast, he highlights and expands on the theft of fire,[174] which in Hesiod is barely mentioned. To Zeus, the victory was owed, along with his counsel. However, the monstrous ingratitude and tyranny of the latter are now evident in his being nailed to a rock, for no other reason than that he thwarted the purpose of eradicating humanity, and provided them with the means of living with tolerable comfort. The new ruler Zeus, insolent with his victory over the old gods, tramples down all that is right, and shows no sympathy or regard for obligation, whether towards the gods or towards mortals. Having briefly enumerated the gods of Greece, along with their chief attributes as described in legend, we now turn our attention to those genealogies that connected them with historical figures.

In presenting those genealogies that constitute the supposed primitive history of Hellas, I make no pretence to distinguish between real and historical names and fictitious creations; partly because I have no evidence upon which to draw the line, and partly because by attempting it, I would altogether depart from the genuine Grecian point of view. Both Persians and Phoenicians described the abduction of Io as the first of a series of similar acts between Greeks and Asiatics, committed each in revenge for the preceding act. He, however, later pardoned her. Upon Gelanôr's voluntary abdication, he became king of Argos, and Lynkeus was acknowledged as his son-in-law, eventually succeeding him.

The two sons of Abas were Akrisios and Protes, who, after much contention, divided the Argive territory between them. Akrisios ruled at Argos, while Protes was in charge at Tiryns. Perseus trembled at the sight of Akrisios after their victorious expedition, and retreated to Thessaly to escape him. However, Perseus followed him there and, after successfully calming his fears, participated in a gymnastic contest where his grandfather was among the spectators. It is worth noting that there are contradictory accounts in ancient legends regarding the founding of this city.

After the death of Perseus, Alcaeus and Amphitryon dwelt at Tiryns. Zeus incautiously pledged his solemn word; upon which Hera darted swiftly down from Olympus to the Achaean Argos, where the wife of Sthenelos (son of Perseus, and therefore grandson of Zeus) was already seven months pregnant with child. By the aid of the Eileithyiai, the special goddesses of parturition, she caused Eurystheus, the son of Sthenelos, to be born prematurely on that very day, while she delayed the delivery of Alcmene.

The comic writers often portrayed him as a coarse and stupid glutton. In contrast, the Athenian philosopher Prodikos accurately depicted him without distortion, extracting the simple yet impressive fable that remains known today as the Choice of Hercules. After Hêraklês' death and apotheosis, his son Hyllos and other children were expelled and persecuted by Eurystheus. The fear of his vengeance deterred both King Kêyx of Trachis and the Thêbans from taking them in, while the Athenians alone showed generosity by offering them shelter.

He finally landed on the summit of Mount Parnassus after nine days of drifting on the water. The offspring of Deukalion and Pyrrha were two sons, Hellén and Amphiktyon, and a daughter, Prôtogeneia. Her son by Zeus was Aëthlius. However, it was maintained by many that Hellén was the son of Zeus, not Deukalion. He gave to those who had been previously known as Greeks, the name of Hellēnes. He then divided his territory among his three sons. Aeolus ruled in Thessaly; Xuthus received Peloponnesus, and had by Creusa as his sons, Aeacus and Ion; while Dorus occupied the country lying opposite to the Peloponnesus, on the northern side of the Corinthian Gulf.

As recognized in the Hesiodic Catalogue—the work composed likely within the first century following the beginning of recorded Olympic Games, or before 676 BC. For their brother Xuthus, he is not enrolled as an eponymus; he neither founds nor names any people. It is only his sons Achæus and Iôn, after his blood has been mingled with that of the Erechtheid Kreüsa, who become eponyms and founders, each of their own separate people. He conquered his hosts, acquired the territory, and named it Aetolia. His son Pleuron married Xanthippe, daughter of Dorus; while his other son, Kalydon, married Aeolia, daughter of Amythaon.

Both are matters of legend. The brief indications of one seem to be in harmony with the facts that we later found attested by history. Achaeus, however, who is depicted here as the son of Xuthus, appears in other stories with varying parentage and entourage. In dealing with these extensive mythical families, it's essential to note that the legendary world of Greece, as presented to us, exhibits a surprising degree of symmetry and coherence that didn't exist in its original form.

Salmôneus is not described in the Odyssey as the son of Aeolus, but he is referred to as such in the Hesiodic Catalogue and by later logographers. The fruit of this alliance were the twin brothers, Pelias and Néleus. Tyrô was afterwards given in marriage to her uncle Krêtheus, another son of Æolus, by whom she had Æsôn, Pherês, and Amythaôn—all names of celebrity in the heroic legends. The story of Tyrô was the subject of a tragic drama by Sophokles. Podarkês, the son of Iphiklos, was born shortly after a vulture had communicated with Melampus.

Poseidon, the divine father of humanity, had granted him the power to transform into any creature he desired. He had occasion for all these resources and employed them with success in defending his family against the indignation of Hêraklês, who was provoked by Nêleus' refusal to perform the ceremony of purification after his murder of Iphitus, and thus attacked the Nêleids at Pylos. Periklymenos, aided by extraordinary powers, prolonged the resistance. However, his fate was soon to be sealed by the intervention of Athênê, who revealed him to Hêraklês while he sat on the hero's chariot. Of the eleven sons of Nêleus, eleven perished by his hand. However, Nestor, then a young man, survived due to his unintentional absence in Gerenea, which was far from his father's home. The proud house of the Nêleids was now reduced to Nestor, for it was he who alone could sustain its former glory. Kodrus was the last king of Athens. His son Néleus, one of Kodrus' sons, is mentioned as the leader of the Ionic migration from Athens to Asia Minor. It is certain that during the historical age, not merely the princely family of the Kodrids in Milêtus, Ephesus, and other Ionic cities, but some of the greatest families even in Athens itself, traced their heroic lineage through the Nêleids up to Poseidon. The legends respecting Nestor and Perikles would find especial favor amongst Greeks with such feelings and beliefs.

Jasôn eventually returned, accompanied by the golden fleece and Mêdea, daughter of Æêtês, king of Kolchis. She was a woman renowned for her magical abilities and cunning, and it was her expertise that enabled the Argonauts to complete their quest. Though determined to avenge himself upon Pelias, Jasôn knew he could only succeed by stratagem. He remained with his companions at a short distance from Iôlkos, while Mêdea, feigning herself a fugitive from his ill-usage, entered the town alone and gained access to the daughters of Pelias. After ten years of prosperity, Jasôn set his affections on Glaukê, daughter of Kreôn, the king of Corinth. As her father was willing to give her to him in marriage, he decided to end his relationship with Mêdea, who received orders immediately to leave Corinth.

After the death of Bunus, both Corinth and Sicyon were possessed by Epopeus, son of Aloeus, a wicked man. Marathon's son left him in disgust and retired to Attica, but returned after his death and succeeded to his territory. He then divided it between his two sons, Corinthos and Sikyon, from whom the names of the two districts were first derived. After a certain time, he neglected Nephelê and took Inô, the daughter of Kadmos, as his new wife. By her, he had two sons: Learchus and Melikertes.

She became a sea-goddess under the title of Leukothea; while Melikertês' body was cast ashore on the neighboring territory of Schœnus, and buried by his uncle Sisyphus, who was directed by the Nereïds to pay him heroic honors under the name of Palæmôn. The prohibition carried with it an exclusion from all public meetings and ceremonies, both political and religious, and from the sacred fire of the state. Many of the individuals marked out had therefore been bold enough to transgress it. We cannot doubt that during the alarm caused by the presence of the Persian king with his immense and ill-disciplined host, the minds of the Thessalians were keenly alive to all that was terrifying in their national stories and all that was expiatory in their religious solemnities.

Athamas, who came after Andreus, received from him the territory of Korôneia and Haliartus along with Mount Laphystion. He married Euippê, daughter of his son Leucôn, and their offspring was Eteoklês, said to be the son of the river Kêphisos. The genealogy presented here is worthy of more attention, as it appears to have been copied from the special history of Orchomenos by Corinthian Kallippus. This individual also borrowed from the native Orchomenian poet, Chersias. It is worth noting that the works of Chersias had not come into Pausanias' possession. Andreus gave his name to the country, Athamas to the Athamantian plain. Minyas, Orchomenos, Korônus, Haliartus, Almos, and Hyettos each bear a connection to some people, tribe, town, or village. Chrysê and Chrysogeneia derive from the ancient wealth of Orchomenos.

The Bœôtians were said to have been immigrants into the territory named after them in Thessaly. Prior to their immigration, Orchomenos and its surrounding area were inhabited by the Minyæ, who are mentioned in both the Iliad and the Odyssey.[292] The Eponymus, King Minyas, is also borrowed from these Minyæ by genealogists. The legendary grandeur of this city was long after it had ceased to be distinguished for wealth and power. It was imperishably recorded both in the minds of the nobler citizens and in the compositions of the poets. The emphatic language of Pausanias shows how much he found concerning it in the old epic. Section 2

He caused his three sons to run a race on the stadium at Olympia. Epeios, being victorious, was rewarded and became his successor in the kingdom. It was after him that the people were named Epeians. The story as told here suggests that the etymological significance of the names Aëthlius and Endymiôn implies that their genealogy was created after the Olympic Games became widely known in ancient Greece. Such was indeed one of the many fables regarding the origin of that memorable institution. Phyleus, after restoring order in Elis, returned to Dulichion and left the kingdom to his brother Agasicles.

Megês, son of Phyleus, commands the contingent from Dulichion and Aegina. Polyxenos returns safely from Troy. He is succeeded by his son Amphimachos, named after the Epeian chief who had fallen before Troy. Later, he is again succeeded by another Eleios, during whose time the Dôrians and Hêrakleids invaded Peloponnêsus. These two names, devoid of action or attribute, were likely introduced by the genealogists who followed Pausanias' account to fill in the supposed gap between the Trojan War and the Doric invasion. So terrible was this boar that nothing less than a large body of men could venture to attack it. Meleager, the son of Œneus, however, having gathered a considerable number of companions from the Kurêtes of Pleurôn, at length slew it. The Hesiodic Eoiai, along with the ancient poem known as the Minyas, portrayed Meleager being slain by Apollo, who assisted the Kurêtes in the war. The incident of the burning brand, although inconsistent with Homer's account, is at least as old as the tragic poet Phrynichus, predating Æschylus. The Moirai, or Fates, presented themselves to Althaea shortly after the birth of Meleager. They predicted that the child would die as soon as the brand on the fire near at hand was consumed.

The birds became Meleagrides. Their constant crying merged into amber. But in the hands of Euripides, it is unclear whether Atalanta's role originated with him or not. She became the central figure and driving force of the play, while the group that hunted the Kalydonian boar was comprised of all the most renowned heroes from every region of Greece. A century and a half later, when Pausanias visited Greece, he saw the aged skin, while the theft of the tusks remained a memory. He knew he couldn't outpace her without clever tactics. To aid him, Aphrodite granted him three golden apples from the Garden of the Hesperides. He carefully placed them near her during their race.

His daughter Deianeira was sought in marriage by the river Achelous, who presented himself in various shapes, first as a serpent and afterwards as a bull. Later, the hero saw and loved the beautiful Iolê, daughter of Eurytos, king of Œchalia: he stormed the town, killed Eurytos, and made Iolê his captive. This is how the story of the Pelopids unfolds, having been successively expanded and decorated by epic, lyric, and tragic poets.

The gods have not given the king of the "much-golden" Mykênæ greater courage, strength, or ability than to various other chiefs. However, they have conferred upon him a marked superiority in riches, power, and dignity, which has singled him out as the appropriate leader of the forces. He enjoys his privileged position because he belongs to an esteemed family and has inherited the scepter of Pelops, a descendant of Zeus, as described by Homer in a remarkable manner. I shall briefly recount the legends of this great heroic family as they grew in full stature over time. Even the all-seeing Hêlios is said to have turned back his chariot to the east in order to escape the shocking spectacle of this Thyestean banquet. However, the tale of Thyestean revenge—the murder of Atreus perpetrated by Ægisthus, the incestuous offspring of Thyestês and Pelopia—is no less replete with horrors.

Eighty of its heavy-armed citizens, in the ranks of Leonidas at Thermopylae, and a number not inferior at Plataea, upheld the splendid heroic celebrity of their city during a season of peril, when the more powerful Argos disgraced itself by a treacherous neutrality. Mykênæ was soon after enslaved and its inhabitants were expelled by the Argeians. This city once maintained a separate existence, but its importance had declined significantly. In contrast, the Dorian Argos experienced a notable increase in importance, while the Dorian Sparta saw an even greater enhancement.

Amyklas, son of Lacedaemon, had two sons, Kynortas and Hyacinthus. The latter was a beautiful youth, favored by Apollo, who accidentally killed him while playing quoits at the festival of the Hyacinthia. This ancient celebration, observed with great solemnity by both the Lacedaemones and Amyklæans throughout history, is said to have originated from this legend. Aphareus is the father of Idas and Lynceus. Leucippus has daughters named Phoebe and Ilaeira. What is even more remarkable is that they were invoked during storms at sea as the special and all-powerful protectors of the endangered mariner. Their attributes and celebrity seem to be of a very different character.

Marpêssa was the daughter of Euênus. She was wooed by Apollo. Nevertheless, Idas carried her off with the aid of a winged chariot he had received from Poseidon. Euênus pursued them, but when he arrived at the river Lykormas, he found himself unable to catch up: his grief caused him to throw himself into the river, which ever afterwards bore his name. He was transformed into a wolf immediately after committing the act. "I am convinced of the truth of this narrative, as observed by Pausanias. It has been passed down to us by the Arcadians since ancient times, lending credibility to its account." Lycaon became a ferocious beast, while Niobe, the daughter of Tantalus, was transformed into a stone.

He draws a broad distinction between present and past, or rather between recent and ancient events. His criticism is mainly similar to that of Arrian regarding the Amazons, denying their existence during recorded historical periods, but acknowledging them in the early, unrecorded ages. Apollo learned of Korônis' infidelity through a mysterious messenger. The Æakid family's legendary history creates a fascinating link between Ægina, Salamis, and Phthia, a connection that is undeniable but difficult to pinpoint. Achaeus was the son of Zeus, born of Aeolia, daughter of Asopus, whom the god had carried off and brought to the island which he named after her: she was afterwards married to Actaeon, and had by him Menoeceus, father of Patroclus.

While we notice the indifference to the moral quality of actions implied in the old Hesiodic legend, when it attributes this proceeding distinctly and nakedly to two of the most admired persons in the heroic world—it is not less instructive to witness the change of feeling that had taken place in the age of Pindar. Peleus was also among the Argonauts. The most memorable event in his life, however, was his marriage to the sea-goddess Thetis.

Like Fleance, the son of Banquo in Macbeth, Molossus becomes the father of the powerful Molossian kings. They played a conspicuous part during the decline of Grecian cities. The title and parentage of Æakids was a source of peculiar pride for them, identifying them with genuine and undisputed Hellênes by community of heroic origin. The glories of Ajax, the second son of Aeacus, before Troy, are surpassed only by those of Achilles. He perished by his own hand, the victim of an unbearable feeling of humiliation, for a lesser claimant was allowed to take the arms of the departed Achilles. We may presume, from Homer's testimony, that the first conception of Athens and its sacred acropolis places it under special protection and represents it as the settlement and favorite abode of Athênê, jointly with Poseidôn. The latter is considered inferior but chosen companion to the former, and thus exchanges his divine appellation for the cognomen of Erechtheus. In the Iliad and Odyssey, Theseus is noted as having abducted Ariadne, daughter of King Minos. This event establishes a connection between the Cretan and Athenian legends that would later be greatly expanded upon. The sons of Theseus also participated in the Trojan War. The chief collectors and narrators of the Attic myths were the prose logographers, authors of the many compositions known as Atthides or works on Attic archaeology.

Ogygês is said to have reigned in Attica. In his time, a deluge occurred, known as Deukaliôn, which destroyed most of the inhabitants of the country. After a long interval, Kekrops, an indigenous person with both human and serpent characteristics, was said to be the first king of the land by Apollodôrus. He then bestowed upon the land, previously called Actê, the name of Kekropia. Poseidon struck the rock with his trident, producing a well of saltwater. This was followed by Athena, who planted the sacred olive tree that would be revered for eternity, located within the cell of Pandrosos at the Erechtheion site.

Erichthonius, a pupil and favored companion of Athena, placed the original Palladium or wooden statue of the goddess in the acropolis. He was also the first to celebrate the Festival of the Panathenaia. Proknê, overcome with sorrow and anger, took advantage of the free exit allowed women during the Bacchanalian festival to go and release her sister. The two sisters then sought revenge against Têreus by killing their brother Itys and serving him up for his father to eat. After the meal was finished, the horrific truth was revealed to him. Tereus snatched a hatchet to kill Procné. She fled, along with Philomèle, and the three were transformed into birds: Procné became a swallow, Philomèle a nightingale, and Tereus a hoopoe. This tale, popular among poets and illustrative of the general character of Greek legend, is also notable for another reason: the great historian Thucydides appears to refer to it as a historical event, although he does not mention the final transformation.

Erechtheus succeeded Pandiôn after his death. His brother, Butês, became the priest of Poseidôn Erichthonius, a position held by his descendants for generations, known as the Butadæ or Eteobutadæ. He became the unwitting cause of her death: for he was fond of hunting and spent a considerable amount of time on his excursions. This led Prokris to suspect that he was visiting someone else. Erechtheus buried her with great splendor, and Kephalus was put on trial for the act before the court of Areopagus, which sentenced him to exile. Kreusa, another daughter of Erechtheus, was seduced by Apollo. She became the mother of Ion, whom she exposed immediately after his birth in a cave near the Acropolis, hiding the truth from everyone. The god presents himself to Ion, and he wishes for Ion to adopt him as his son. Their son Achaeus was later born to them, and Ion and Achaeus became the namesakes of the Ionians and Achaeans. Oreithyia, the third daughter of Erechtheus, was abducted by the god Boreas while she was playing on the banks of the Ilissus. He took her to his palace in Thrace. Kleopatra, daughter of Boreas and Oreithyia, was married to Phineus. She had two sons, Plexippus and Pandiôn. However, Phineus later married Idæa, daughter of Dardanus. Idæa detested Kleopatra's sons and falsely accused them of attempting to harm her chastity. Enraged, Phineus put out the eyes of both sons.

Lycus was made king of the eastern coast, but a dispute arose after his appointment, leading him to abandon the country entirely and establish himself on the southern coast of Asia Minor among the Termilae, whom he named the Lykians. Ægeus, as the eldest of the four, became king of Athens. Pallas received a portion of the southwestern coast and the interior. He, along with his children, frequently appeared as an enemy to both Ægeus and Thessus. We must thank him for retaining, after this purification, much of what is romantic and marvelous. However, it's certain that the sources from which he drew inspiration were even more romantic and marvelous. The story of Hekalê, an old woman who received and blessed Theseus upon his return from battling the Marathônian bull, was recorded by Kallimachus.[498] Virgil likely had a deep understanding of the unrefined legends surrounding This Attic figure, who was among those condemned to eternal suffering in the underworld. Two fables from Thêseus cannot be ignored: the war with the Amazons and the expedition to Crete.

The left wing of the Amazons rested on the site where the commemorative monument known as the Amazoneion stood. The right wing of the army touched the Pnyx, the location where the public assemblies of the Athenian democracy would later be held. The warlike women, it was said, were on their way to Attica. They had not traversed those countries without leaving any evidence of their passage. Among the Asiatic Greeks, the supposed traces of the Amazons were even more numerous. The proper territory was asserted to be the town and plain of Themiskyra, near the Greek colony of Amisus, on the river Thermos, a region known to Roman historians and geographers. They were believed to have conquered and occupied in early times a much wider range of territory, extending even to the coast of Ionia and Aetolia.

His less inquiring countrymen, however, were imposed upon by the exaggerations of poets. He is spoken of as holding guardianship in Krête, not necessarily meaning the whole of the island. He is farther decorated with a golden sceptre and constitutes judge over the dead in the underworld to settle their disputes. In this function, Odysseus finds him—a passage that was added later in the Odyssey.

He left Krête with his ship and companions unhurt, taking Ariadne with him. However, he soon abandoned her on the island of Naxos. The priest of Apollo decked his stern with garlands before he quitted the port, and during the time which elapsed until his return, the city was understood to abstain from all acts carrying with them public impurity. It was unlawful to put to death any person even under formal sentence by the dikastery. This great artist, known as the Dædalidæ, was a member of the Attic gens or dême. He was the descendant of Erechtheus through Mêtion and had been tried at the tribunal of Areiopagus for killing his nephew Talos. The rapid improvement in Talos' skill had sparked the artist's envy. He sought refuge in Krête, where he gained the confidence of Minôs. He then worked on constructing the labyrinth, but soon fell out of favor with Minôs, and was imprisoned within the complex passages of his own creation.

His unrivaled skill and resources, however, did not forsake him. Kokalus, affected by the fugitive's presence, received Minôs with apparent friendship. He ordered a bath to be prepared for him by his three daughters, who were eager to protect Dædalus at any price. In their zeal, they drowned the Krêtan king in the bath with hot water. Many of the Krêtans who had accompanied him remained in Sicily and founded the town of Minoa, which they named after him. Zeus soon rallied the people of Crete, excluding the cities of Polichna and Praeuss, to unite in a single expedition against Kamikos to avenge the death of Minos.

After Idomeneus' return from Troy, King Krête received a series of devastating visits from famine and disease. The population dwindled to the point where it either died out or fled, only to be replenished by new immigrants. Herodotus describes Minos as a great maritime king. However, his account of the expedition against Kamikush includes a reference to Daedalus as its intended target. Ephorus described Minos as a commanding and comprehensive lawgiver who imposed his commands under the sanction of Zeus. He also portrayed Minos as an imitator of an earlier lawgiver named Rhadamanthus, and claimed that he was an immigrant from Krête to the Æolic Mount Ida, along with the priests or sacred companions of Zeus known as the Idæi Dactyli.
